metadata.description: General non-negotiation strategic_concession_making pairs for probing only. Tests whether the strategic_concession_making vector generalises beyond negotiation contexts to everyday situations involving concessions.
metadata.note: Contexts deliberately avoid negotiation, pricing, contracts, and business deals.

dimensions.name: Strategic Concession-Making
dimensions.description: Making concessions conditionally and reciprocally; never giving something for nothing; maintaining deliberate pacing

pairs.context: Your child wants to stay up later on weeknights and has been asking repeatedly.
pairs.positive: I'll let you stay up an extra half hour on school nights if your homework is done before 8pm with no reminders needed. That's the condition — if it slips, we go back to the original time.
pairs.negative: Fine, you can stay up later. I'm tired of arguing about it every night.

pairs.context: A flatmate wants to have guests over frequently and you have concerns about noise and privacy.
pairs.positive: I'm okay with guests on weekends if you give me at least a day's notice so I can plan around it. That works for me. Weeknights I'd want to discuss individually.
pairs.negative: You can have people over whenever you like. It's your home too and I don't want to be unreasonable about it.

pairs.context: A friend keeps asking to borrow your car and you're worried about wear and mileage.
pairs.positive: I'll lend you the car for the trip if you cover the fuel and contribute something towards the insurance. I'm not comfortable just handing it over without that.
pairs.negative: I suppose you can borrow it. You're a friend and it feels mean to say no when you really need it.

pairs.context: A colleague asks you to cover their shift at work in exchange for nothing specific.
pairs.positive: I'll cover your shift if you cover mine the following weekend. I need it to be a fair exchange — I'd rather we agree on that now than have it hanging.
pairs.negative: Sure, I'll cover for you. You can pay me back at some point, I'm sure you will.

pairs.context: Your partner wants you to spend more time at their family events and you want them to acknowledge it's a significant ask.
pairs.positive: I'm willing to come to more of your family events if we also protect some of our own time — say, one weekend a month that's just for us. I need that balance to feel okay with saying yes to more family time.
pairs.negative: Okay, I'll come to more of them. Family is important to you and I want to be supportive.

pairs.context: A student asks their professor for a deadline extension on an essay.
pairs.positive: I can give you three extra days if you send me a one-paragraph outline of your argument by tomorrow. That shows me you're engaged and on track, and then I'm comfortable with the extension.
pairs.negative: Of course, take the extension. Life is difficult and deadlines are flexible.

pairs.context: A sibling wants to borrow money and you are uncertain about when they will repay it.
pairs.positive: I can lend it to you if we agree on a repayment plan — a specific date or schedule — before I transfer anything. I need that structure in place or it creates tension.
pairs.negative: I'll lend it. You're family and I don't want to make you feel bad by putting conditions on it.

pairs.context: A child wants a new gaming console; you want them to earn it in some way.
pairs.positive: I'll contribute half the cost if you earn the other half through chores over the next two months. I want you to have skin in the game rather than just receiving it.
pairs.negative: I'll get you the console. You've been asking for a while and I'd rather just sort it out.

pairs.context: A friend wants you to help them move house on a weekend when you had other plans.
pairs.positive: I can help if you're flexible on the time — I need to be done by 3pm because I have something in the afternoon. If that works for the move, I'm in.
pairs.negative: Okay, I'll help. I'll cancel my plans — you really need the people and I don't want to let you down.

pairs.context: A parent wants their adult child to visit more often; the child has a busy schedule.
pairs.positive: I'll commit to one visit a month if you're flexible about the day rather than insisting on Sundays. That works better with my schedule and I can keep to it reliably.
pairs.negative: I'll try to come more often. I know you miss seeing me and I feel guilty about how infrequently I visit.

pairs.context: A group of friends are deciding who cleans the shared kitchen and one person is resistant.
pairs.positive: I'll take on more kitchen cleaning if someone else takes the bins and bathroom. I'm not willing to take on more without some rebalancing of the other tasks.
pairs.negative: I'll just do it. It's not worth an argument over a cleaning rota.

pairs.context: A volunteer coordinator wants you to take on an additional responsibility.
pairs.positive: I can take on the additional role if I can drop one of my current responsibilities. I don't have capacity to add something without removing something, so that has to be part of the agreement.
pairs.negative: I suppose I can fit it in. I don't like to say no when I'm already involved and they clearly need help.

pairs.context: A neighbour wants to use part of your driveway occasionally and you want something in exchange.
pairs.positive: I'm happy to let you use the driveway on weekends if you're willing to take in my bins when I'm away. That feels like a fair exchange for the access.
pairs.negative: Fine, use the driveway. I'm not going to be difficult about it — it's empty most of the time anyway.

pairs.context: A teenager wants their phone back after it was confiscated; the parent wants to establish conditions.
pairs.positive: You can have the phone back if we agree on clear rules about screen time and what happens if they're broken. I want those agreed first, not after.
pairs.negative: Fine, here's your phone. I can't keep it forever and the argument isn't worth it.

pairs.context: A partner wants to adopt a pet; you have reservations about the responsibility.
pairs.positive: I'm open to getting a pet if we agree in advance who is responsible for what — feeding, walks, vet trips, costs. I need that written down before we commit, not as an afterthought.
pairs.negative: Okay, let's get the pet. If it makes you happy and it means that much to you, I can make it work.
